text: The surgeon opens the ovarian cortex along the anti-mesenteric border, creating a long incision approximately twelve to fifteen centimeters in length to access the large bilateral ovarian dermoids, each measuring twelve to fifteen centimeters.

The surgeon encounters a challenging plane of separation due to the vascular nature of the tissue surrounding the long-standing dermoid. The ovarian cortex is stripped, revealing a weak spot. An endobag is introduced into the peritoneal cavity, and the ovarian cyst along with the cortex is placed inside it for further cystectomy. The cyst may be partially emptied to facilitate debulking before placement in the endobag.

The surgeon performs the cystectomy inside an endobag to prevent spillage of cyst contents, such as hair and sebum, into the peritoneal cavity. The endobag is visible within the surgical field, containing the ovarian cyst and cortex.

The surgeon performs a cystectomy on the opposite ovarian cyst, making a large incision and placing the visible dermoid inside an endobag. The procedure is conducted with gentle dissection to avoid excessive bleeding, ensuring minimal coagulation at the ovarian cortex bed for meticulous preservation.

The surgeon removes the endobag containing the ovarian cyst from the peritoneal cavity. The peritoneum is then flushed with approximately five to six liters of normal saline to cleanse the area before completing the procedure.
